title: The plugin that fixed my Claude Code workflow

eval.summary: Short walkthrough of the GSD (Get Shit Done) Claude Code plugin. Core workflow is three commands: 'gsd new project' triggers deep discovery questions and optionally spawns four parallel research agents to generate a phased roadmap; 'gsd plan [phase]' creates a detailed execution plan for a specific phase; 'gsd execute [phase]' runs that phase. The presenter frames it as a structured planning layer on top of Claude Code to prevent missed steps in app or automation builds. Not designed for quick tasks — intended for multi-phase project work. Link promised in comments.

title: Use these 3 tricks to reduce your Claude code token usage.

eval.summary: Three token-efficiency tricks for Claude Code: (1) Use /model opus plan to route planning to Opus and execution to Sonnet, reducing Opus token burn on coding tasks. (2) Use /compact when conversations get long — Claude re-reads full history on every message, so compacting compresses to a summary and extends the session. (3) Append 'ultra think' to prompts before complex tasks to force full problem analysis before code generation. Video is a short teaser for a longer 3-hour Claude Code course the creator is promoting via DM.

patterns.description: Route planning to stronger model (Opus), execution to faster/cheaper model (Sonnet) via explicit model-tier switching — not leaving model choice to default
patterns.apply_to: orchestrator.py task routing logic — when spinning up agent subtasks, classify as plan vs. execute and assign model tier accordingly; document in CLAUDE.md

patterns.description: Context window accumulation is a silent token cost — every message re-reads full history; proactively compress/summarize long sessions before they hit limits rather than reacting after
patterns.apply_to: session-log.md protocol and orchestrator session management — add a token-count checkpoint that triggers summary compression at a threshold (e.g. 80k tokens); could be a scheduled n8n step

patterns.description: 'Ultra think' (or equivalent extended reasoning trigger phrase) prepended to complex prompts forces full problem decomposition before output generation, reducing mid-task corrections and wasted tokens
patterns.apply_to: CLAUDE.md system prompt conventions and agent task templates — add a standard prefix pattern for high-complexity tasks; document which task types warrant it

patterns.description: Model tiering by task phase (plan/execute/review) is a repeatable architectural pattern — not just a Claude Code trick but a general orchestration principle
patterns.apply_to: orchestrator_v2.py task dispatch — add task_phase field to task schema and map phases to model tiers in config rather than hardcoding

stack_evaluation.replaces_or_complements: pattern only — no new tool; complements existing orchestrator.py + Claude API usage patterns
stack_evaluation.reasoning: Lens 1 drives this verdict entirely. All three techniques are immediately applicable to the Veritas stack without new tooling. The /compact pattern maps directly to session-log.md compression strategy; model tiering maps to orchestrator task dispatch; ultra think maps to CLAUDE.md prompt conventions. Zero integration cost, measurable token reduction expected.
action_items: Add 'ultra think' (or 'think step by step through the full problem before responding') as a documented prefix pattern in CLAUDE.md for complex agent tasks
Add token threshold check to orchestrator session loop — trigger context compression/summarization at ~80% context window
Add task_phase classification (plan|execute|review) to orchestrator task schema and map to model selection config

strategic_signal.signal: Token cost management is becoming a top operator concern at the Claude Code level — Veritas should ensure AgentOS surfaces per-session token burn metrics in the React dashboard so operators can tune model routing and session hygiene proactively rather than hitting limits unexpectedly.

title: Claude Code skill-building tips shift #claudecode #skillbuilding #aiworkflows

eval.summary: A Claude Code engineer shares three skill-building tips: (1) Add a 'gotchas' section to each skill — a running list of mistakes Claude has made that you don't want repeated, which acts as iterative improvement over time. (2) Structure skills as folders, not single markdown files — include scripts, references, examples, and templates alongside the prompt so Claude can pull them in on demand without polluting the context window. (3) Avoid over-specifying instructions (railroading) — give Claude context and constraints but let it adapt, since rigid instructions cause Claude to struggle. The tips are framed around building reusable Claude Code skills that improve through use.

patterns.description: Add a GOTCHAS section to each skill/CLAUDE.md that logs specific mistakes Claude has made — acts as a persistent correction layer that improves the skill over time without rewriting core instructions
patterns.apply_to: Every skill file and CLAUDE.md — add '## Gotchas' section and update it whenever Claude makes a repeatable mistake during a session

patterns.description: Structure skills as folders (not flat markdown files) containing the prompt, supporting scripts, reference docs, templates, and examples — Claude pulls files in on demand, keeping context window clean
patterns.apply_to: .lux/skills/ or equivalent skill storage — convert any existing flat skill .md files to folder structure with subfiles Claude can reference lazily

patterns.description: Anti-railroading principle: write skill instructions as context + constraints rather than step-by-step procedures — over-specification causes Claude to fail when edge cases arise
patterns.apply_to: All CLAUDE.md skill definitions and orchestrator prompt templates — audit existing instructions for over-prescription and replace rigid steps with intent + boundaries

stack_evaluation.replaces_or_complements: pattern only — no tool. Directly improves CLAUDE.md design and .lux/ skill architecture
stack_evaluation.reasoning: Lens 1 drives this verdict entirely. All three patterns are immediately applicable to the existing Veritas stack — particularly the gotchas section (previously flagged as high-value) and folder-as-skill structure which directly maps to .lux/Data/ architecture. No tool evaluation needed.
